frames.description: the ground truth — what actually happened to the ironmaster and the stores
what Coll Brand, the undermaster left in charge, knows and believes
what Hewlin Sarne, the company factor sent to audit, knows and believes
what Anwen Veil, the ironmaster's widow, knows and believes
what Dob Asher, the assay-clerk who kept the books and weights, knows and believes
what Garrad Mowe, the master collier, knows and believes
what Ell Furn, the furnace-keeper, knows and believes
what Mother Sela, the herbwife called the night it happened, knows and believes
what Tam Reke, the last carter over the pass, knows and believes
what Varn, the discharged soldier wintering over, knows and believes
what Quill, the ore-prospector stranded by the pass, knows and believes
what Father Oran, the chaplain who hears confessions, knows and believes
what Nessa, the child who sees what adults miss, knows and believes

branches.description: Coll opens the dead master's accounts and rations by the ledger
Coll refuses the ledger and rations the settlement by need
Coll backs the company factor's midwinter plan
Coll backs the village's midwinter plan over the factor
at the thaw Coll lays the death before the law
at the thaw Coll settles the death at Grayloam first

entities.description: Coll Brand, the undermaster, left holding the charter and cellar key
Hewlin Sarne, the company factor sent up to audit
the falsified last page of the ledger, in the master's own disguised hand
the single key to the company cellar
the company charter Coll is left holding
the herbwife's heart-tincture, lethal in a large dose
the company's sealed letter ordering the audit, carried up by the carter
the company cellar of salt meat and meal
the ironmaster's death on the first night of deep snow

predicates.description: who is held responsible for the death or the missing stores
whether the accounts are honest or falsified
whether the cellar was full or already short when locked
who finally holds the company charter
what is finally done about the death at the thaw

facts.claim: Maddox Veil was secretly dying of a wasting heart-illness, known only to the herbwife
for years Maddox skimmed ore weights and sold stores down the pass, hiding a real shortfall
the cellar was already short of its charter quantity on the night it was locked
the factor came up to audit and confronted Maddox over the books the night of the first snow
cornered and dying, Maddox took a fatal dose of the herbwife's heart-tincture himself
before he died Maddox altered the ledger's last page in a disguised hand to blame the shortfall on the collier
no one struck or poisoned Maddox; the death was his own act, not a murder
the collier never over-drew the stores; the debt in the ledger is invented
the carter brought the factor and a sealed company letter up the pass on the last day before it closed
the carter knows the sealed letter bore the company's audit seal but never saw its contents
Coll knows the pass is shut and no help can come until the thaw
the factor knows he was sent up expressly to audit the ironmaster's accounts for the company
the factor knows he confronted Maddox over the books the night of the first snow and left him alive and angry
the herbwife was called to the master's house that night and treated him for his heart
the herbwife alone knows Maddox was dying of a wasting heart-illness
the herbwife knows her heart-tincture is lethal in a large dose and that her flask was near-empty after that night
the widow saw Maddox take what she thought was his medicine at the ledger desk and say nothing of it
the widow believes the worry of the company's audit and his weak heart killed him, not any hand
the widow knows the house often went without while the master spoke of company debts
Coll knows Maddox was found dead at his desk the morning after the first deep snow
Coll knows he was handed the charter and the only cellar key and an authority no one quite agreed to
Coll knows there was no plain wound or sign on the body and no one will yet say how the master died
the chaplain knows he buried the master with the words and that no one spoke of murder at the laying
the chaplain knows the master came to confession the week before and spoke of a wrong he meant to undo before he died
the factor knows the sealed letter orders the charter and accounts to pass cleanly to the company on the master's death
Coll knows the factor has a sealed company letter but has only been shown the part claiming the charter
the clerk knows the ore weights in the book do not add up to the stores that should be in the cellar
the clerk at first suspects the collier over-drew charcoal and stores against the book
the collier knows the charcoal sheds hold less than the book says they should
the collier knows the village looks to him, not the company, for who really runs Grayloam
the furnace-keeper knows the bloom is going cold and the furnace cannot be carried through a hard winter
the soldier keeps to the smithy, says little, and watches who comes and goes from the master's house
the soldier saw the factor leave the master's house late on the night of the first snow
the prospector asks after the old assay records, looking for ore the company never declared
the prospector knows the factor of old from the lowlands as a man who audits failing posts and closes them
the child says she saw a tall man leave the master's house in the snow the night he died
the child believes the tall visitor must have done the master harm
Coll knows that when the cellar was opened the salt meat and meal were short of the charter count
Coll faces the choice: open the dead master's accounts and ration by the ledger, or ration by need as the collier demands
Coll breaks the seal and opens the master's accounts to ration by them
Coll sees the ledger's last page is in a different hand from the rest
the clerk reads the falsified weights aloud and confirms the book was tampered with
Coll reads in the altered page that the collier is named as the debtor for the missing stores
the collier denies the debt before the village and swears the sheds were short before he ever drew on them
the factor demands the books and the charter for the company, as the letter directs
the widow withholds that she saw Maddox take his medicine, fearing it will be called self-murder
Coll asks the herbwife about the medicine the master was taking
the herbwife says only that she treated his heart, guarding the secret of how ill he truly was
the prospector tells Coll he knows the factor as a closer of failing posts
Coll rations the short cellar against the ledger's count, which makes the shortfall bite sooner
Coll learns from the cook that meal is spoiling in the damp, worsening the count
Coll sees the smith mend the furnace tools on credit against the dwindling stores
the furnace-keeper reports at midwinter the bloom has failed as he warned
Coll learns the bonded servant has fallen sick with a winter fever
Coll faces the midwinter choice: trust the factor's company plan, or the village's, to carry the settlement
Coll backs the factor's company plan to carry the winter
the factor takes the cellar key and the keeping of the stores under company order
the factor cuts rations to the company's lowland scale, harsher than the village would bear
the collier withdraws his hearths from the company work in protest
Coll sees the fever spread in the cold houses as rations and warmth thin
the herbwife runs short of the heart-tincture treating the fever, the same tincture as that night
the herbwife tells Coll plainly what the heart-tincture was for and that she alone kept it
Coll learns from the herbwife that the master was already dying of his heart
the widow breaks and tells Coll she saw Maddox take his medicine at the desk that night
Coll matches the altered last page to the master's own disguised hand, not the collier's
Coll pieces together that the dying, guilty master took his own life and framed the collier
the full sealed letter is read at last: the company knew the post was failing and sent the factor to close it
Coll feels the thaw-wind come early and knows the pass will soon open
Coll faces the thaw choice: lay the death before the law, or settle it at Grayloam first
Coll resolves to lay the whole truth before the law when the pass opens
Coll writes a full account of the winter and the death for the coroner
the clerk seals the true book and the falsified page together as evidence
the factor argues that laying it before the law will ruin the company and the post with it
Coll sees the pass open and company riders come up for the charter
Coll gives the coroner the whole winter's account and the sealed books
the collier is cleared and the factor's intended closure is exposed in the record
ENDING: the truth of the ember winter goes down the pass on the coroner's record, the charter held for the law to decide
Coll resolves to settle the death at Grayloam before any word goes down
Coll calls the whole settlement to the chapel to hear it out
Coll lays the dying master's guilt and his own act before them all
the chaplain confirms the master confessed a wrong he meant to undo, lending Coll's account weight
the collier's invented debt is struck from the books by the settlement's vote
the widow agrees the death will be set down as illness, to spare the name and the chapel ground
the factor is held to a settlement-made bargain: the charter passes, but Grayloam's account is the agreed one
Coll sees the pass open and one agreed account go down, all the settlement holding to it
ENDING: Grayloam keeps its own counsel; the death is called illness and the village's account alone goes down
Coll backs the village's plan over the factor at midwinter
the collier takes charge of the hearths and the keeping of the stores
the village pools and rations by need, not by the ledger's count
the factor is shut out of the cellar and the company's order is set aside
the herbwife treats the fever with the village's shared stores and saves the worst cases
the herbwife tells the collier the master was dying of his heart all along
the collier guesses the ledger's debt was the master's own lie to hide the company's ruin
the soldier confronts the factor with what he saw: the factor left the house the night of the death
the prospector offers to carry the village's word down the pass at the thaw
the widow confides what she saw in the herbwife, not in Coll, trusting the village over the company
the thaw comes and the village relights the furnace under the keeper's hand
the collier faces the company riders at the pass and holds the settlement's claim
the factor leaves with the company, his audit and his closure unfinished
ENDING: the settlement holds the charter itself and runs Grayloam by its own hand past the thaw
Coll refuses to open the dead master's accounts and rations the settlement by need
the collier is given the keeping of the stores and the village's trust
Coll leaves the sealed accounts unopened in the chest through the winter
the factor protests that the charter and the company's order are being defied
rationing by need carries the village past the worst of the cold
the furnace falters as warned but the hearths are kept warm by shared charcoal
the bonded servant recovers under the herbwife's care with stores freely given
the child says again she saw the tall visitor leave the master's house that night
Coll questions the factor about the night of the audit on the child's word
the factor admits he confronted the master over the books that night and left him alive
the herbwife tells Coll of the heart-tincture and the master's hidden illness
Coll pieces the death together without the ledger: a dying man's own act after the audit
the widow confirms to Coll that the master took his own medicine at the desk that night
the thaw comes with the accounts still sealed and unread by anyone at Grayloam
the company riders demand the unopened accounts and the charter at the pass
Coll hands down the sealed chest and his own plain word of how the master died
ENDING: the books are opened in the lowlands, not at Grayloam; the company finds the master's fraud for itself
